entries.title: Message editor overview: Pretty / Raw / Hex
entries.body:
# Message editor - the HTTP message editor

The **message editor** is the component in which Burp displays any HTTP or WebSocket request and response. You will find it everywhere: in **Proxy** (Intercept and history), in **Repeater**, in **Intruder**, in **Logger** - anywhere there is a message you need to read or modify. In essence it is your main window for working with raw traffic.

The editor consists of two parts:

- **Text editor** - the message itself (read-only in some tools, editable in **Repeater** and Intercept).
- **Side panel** - this is where **Inspector** and other analysis tools live.

At the top of the editor are the view tabs: **Pretty**, **Raw**, **Hex**, and for responses also **Render**. These are different views of one and the same message.

## Pretty

**Pretty** shows the same message as **Raw**, but with automatic formatting: tidy indentation and line breaks improve the readability of data, markup, and code in the HTTP message. JSON, XML, HTML - all of it is rendered in a neat form. The tab is available when the message contains content that is supported for formatting. This is the default mode and the best choice for everyday reading.

## Raw

**Raw** is the message as it is, without embellishment: exactly the bytes and line breaks that go over the wire. In terms of features it is no worse than **Pretty** (it has syntax highlighting, hotkeys, search), it simply lacks auto-formatting. When to choose **Raw**:

- when the **exact** appearance of the message matters (whitespace, case, order) - for example, in parsing and desync attacks;
- when **Pretty** is "tidying up" something and you need to see the original;
- when you are editing a request and do not want formatting to interfere.

There is a **\n** button - it shows/hides non-printing characters (handy for catching stray whitespace and control bytes).

## Hex

**Hex** shows the message as hexadecimal values, 16 bytes per row. You need it when working at the level of individual bytes:

- to look at the **code point** of a specific character;
- to insert or fix **non-printing** characters (null byte, control characters);
- to precisely insert/delete **bytes**.

The **Hex** context menu has the actions **Insert byte**, **Insert bytes**, **Insert string** and delete. This is your tool for payloads that cannot be typed as ordinary text.

## Render

**Render** applies to responses with HTML or images: Burp tries to render the response roughly the way a browser would show it. This is a rough rendering (without full JS execution and resources), but it is enough to quickly understand what kind of page the server returned and to notice payload reflection.

## Additional tabs

- **GraphQL** - appears automatically for GraphQL requests, separating the query itself from the variables.
- Optional breakdown tabs: **Headers**, **Query params**, **Body params**, **Cookies**, **Attributes** - the same thing **Inspector** shows, but as editor tabs.

## Layout

Three icons in the top right corner switch the arrangement of request and response:

- **horizontal** (side by side, left/right);
- **vertical** (one above the other);
- **combined** (request and response on the same tabs, switched between).

Choose based on your monitor: on a wide one the horizontal layout is convenient, on a narrow one the vertical.

## Key capabilities

- **Actions (context menu).** A right click on the message opens context-aware actions: send to another tool (**Send to Repeater/Intruder/Organizer**), encode/decode, record an issue, and so on.
- **Search.** At the bottom of the editor there is a search bar for the message (see the separate entry on the text editor) with case and regex options.
- **Link with Inspector.** Select characters and you will see their code point (dec/hex) in **Inspector**; you can also edit them there.
- **Conversion.** Via **Convert selection** you have URL, HTML, and Base64 encoding/decoding available right over the selection.
- **Automatic Content-Length.** When editing the body in editable tools, by default Burp recalculates the `Content-Length` header itself so the request stays valid (this behaviour is configurable).

## When to use which mode

```
Pretty  -> reading and ordinary editing (90% of the time)
Raw     -> when the exact appearance of bytes/whitespace/order matters (parsing, desync)
Hex     -> non-printing characters, null bytes, byte-by-byte editing of payloads
Render  -> quick glance at how the response looks as a page/image
```

> Tip: when sending tricky payloads, check which mode you are editing in. **Pretty** may reformat what you entered; for precise attacks switch to **Raw** or **Hex** so Burp does not "improve" your payload.

> Pitfall: in read-only tools (history, **Logger**) the editor will not let you change the message - that is normal. To edit and resend, send the request to **Repeater**.

entries.title: Text editor: search, highlighting, line wrapping
entries.body:
# The text editor inside the Message editor

The text editor is the part of the **message editor** that holds the actual text of the request/response. Besides reading and editing, it provides a set of conveniences for analysis: syntax highlighting, search, line wrapping, display of non-printing characters, and encoding hotkeys.

## Syntax highlighting

Syntax in requests and responses is automatically coloured to highlight interesting elements - parameters, HTML tags, JavaScript, JSON, CSS. When you hover the mouse over an encoded fragment, a tooltip pops up with its decoded value (URL or HTML decode depending on the case). This helps you spot injection points on the fly and understand what is encoded in the message without opening decoders.

## Auto-formatting (Pretty printing)

Supported formats - JSON, XML, HTML, CSS, JavaScript - are automatically rendered in a tidy form with proper indentation. In editable tools (for example, **Repeater**) formatting is applied on the fly right while you edit, so the structure stays readable as you make changes.

## Collapsing inessential headers

The button with the **eye icon** hides/shows inessential HTTP headers. When there are many headers, this removes the visual noise and keeps in view what is actually interesting for exploitation.

## Line wrapping

A toggle button enables wrapping of long lines to the editor width - a line does not run off the right edge but wraps, while line numbering is preserved. Useful for long single-line bodies (minified JS, large JSON/Base64) so you can see everything without horizontal scrolling.

## Non-printing characters

A separate button shows hidden characters - bytes with codes below `0x20`, as well as `0x7F-0xFF`. This is indispensable when you are looking for subtle differences between messages or studying a potential injection: a stray space, tab, CR/LF, or control byte becomes immediately visible.

## Search

At the bottom of the text editor there is a **search bar** - you type into it what to find in the message. As you type, matches are highlighted automatically. The **arrow buttons** move the selection to the previous/next match.

The search has settings (in **Message editor settings**):

- **Case sensitive** - take case into account;
- **Regex** - treat the query as a regular expression rather than a literal string;
- auto-scroll to the first match when new text appears.

Searching within the body is a big help during recon: you search the response for `password`, `token`, `apikey`, `debug`, names of internal hosts - and immediately see anything interesting in a large response.

## Hotkeys

The standard ones work:

- `Ctrl+A` - select all, `Ctrl+C` - copy, `Ctrl+V` - paste, `Ctrl+Z` / `Ctrl+Y` - undo/redo.

And very handy encoding/decoding combinations over the selection:

- `Ctrl+U` - URL-encode, `Ctrl+Shift+U` - URL-decode;
- `Ctrl+H` - HTML-encode, `Ctrl+Shift+H` - HTML-decode;
- `Ctrl+B` - Base64-encode, `Ctrl+Shift+B` - Base64-decode.

(The combinations with `Shift` perform the reverse operation - decoding.)

## Practical scenario

```
1. In Repeater you got a large JSON response -> turn on Pretty
2. Search at the bottom: type secret|token|password, enable Regex -> jump between matches with arrows
3. Found an encoded value -> select it -> Ctrl+Shift+B (Base64-decode) right in the editor
4. You suspect a hidden control character -> turn on display of non-printing characters
5. Minified JS is hard to read -> turn on line wrapping
```

> Tip: the combination of "search with Regex + decoding hotkeys" greatly speeds up parsing responses. Do not copy values into external decoders - select and `Ctrl+Shift+U/H/B` in place.

> Pitfall: encoding hotkeys change the SELECTED text right in the editable message. In **Repeater** this edits your request - if you did not want to change the request itself, first copy the fragment and decode the copy (or view the decode in the tooltip/Inspector).
